turns.content: I'm trying to build a simple async executor but I thought if I just store the futures in a Vec and loop through them, they should all run automatically. Is that how it works?

That's a common first guess, but a plain Vec won't work because you need to track when each future is done without blocking the thread. You actually want to use a VecDeque for your task queue so you can pop items off efficiently and add new ones at the back.
Okay, so I put futures in that deque. How do I know when one is ready? Do I just poll every single future until it returns true?

Polling every future on every iteration would be wasteful and slow down your program significantly. Instead, you only poll the specific ones that have registered a waker via Arc to notify when they're ready.
Got it. So I use Arc to share state between threads? And then I check if any of those wakers are triggered?

Exactly, you wrap the future's readiness flag in an Arc so multiple threads can see updates safely. Your loop will keep polling tasks from your VecDeque and only proceed when a Waker signals completion.
I'm still confused about how I handle the single-threaded executor part. Do I just run this main loop forever?
You do keep a main execution loop running in one thread, continuously checking if any Waker has been notified since your last poll cycle.
So I need to store the futures in that VecDeque and wait for wakers before moving on?
Yes, your executor maintains a single-threaded loop where you poll only those futures whose Waker has been signaled, avoiding unnecessary work.
One more thing - how do I know when the entire set of tasks is finished? Is there a flag I check?
You can track the total number of futures you started and count how many have completed via their Waker. When that counter equals your initial task list size, you know the executor is done.

I'm trying to build a single-threaded executor using a VecDeque of tasks and Arc-wrapped Wakers. My assumption is that I just need to loop through the queue, call waker::wake, then check if any task finished before moving on.

That's a common first guess but it misses how futures actually work. In Rust, a Future is not a function you can just call to get a result; it's an object that implements trait Future and must implement async trait Future::poll so the runtime can drive it forward. You cannot simply wake a future and expect it to return immediately without invoking poll on it.

I see, so I was thinking wakers would just trigger completion automatically? But I need to actually invoke the trait Future::poll method on each task in the VecDeque until one returns Ok(()). Is that right?

Exactly. The executor maintains a VecDeque of active futures, and when you wake any future via Arc<Weak>, it does not finish; it just signals readiness so the next poll() call can make progress toward completion. You must repeatedly invoke trait Future::poll on each task in the queue until one returns Ok(()), then remove that completed task from the VecDeque.
Got it, but what about thread safety? If I'm using Arc<Weak> to notify tasks, how do I safely add or remove futures from the VecDeque without deadlocks?

You should use a Mutex around the VecDeque since it's not Send/Sync by default. The waker itself is typically an Arc<Weak> and you only hold shared references when notifying; when you poll, you take ownership of the future from the mutex temporarily so you can mutate the queue safely without holding the lock for long periods.
That makes sense. But I'm still confused about how to structure the executor loop. Should I poll all futures in a single pass or iterate until none remain?

You must poll only one future per iteration and stop that iteration once it returns Ok(()), then restart the outer loop with the updated VecDeque. Polling multiple futures in a single inner loop is inefficient because you might miss events between polls; the standard pattern is to pick one, poll it until done, remove it, wake other ready ones, then repeat.
Okay, so my logic is: while queue not empty -> pop a future -> while !future.poll() == Ok(()) do nothing -> push back wakers if needed -> remove done from queue. Is that the right flow?

You're close but you don't need to keep polling the same future until completion; instead, each poll() call advances it by one step and returns Ok(()) when fully done. So your inner loop should just be a single trait Future::poll call per iteration. If poll returns Err(WakerPending), that means more work is needed later, so you add back to the queue or schedule another wake.

Ah, I see my mistake now. I was thinking wakers would auto-complete tasks like in a higher-level framework, but in raw Rust I have to manually invoke trait Future::poll and handle WakerPending properly. Thanks for clarifying.

You're welcome. Another common pitfall is forgetting that the executor must be Send so it can move futures between threads if needed; otherwise you'll get compile errors when trying to use Arc<Weak> in a multi-threaded context.
Right, I almost forgot about Send. So my executor struct needs to be 'static or wrapped in an Arc so it can be shared? And what about the trait Future::poll signature itself?

The executor should be a type that implements trait Future and is Send, often implemented as an enum of active tasks. The poll method must return Poll<Output, WakerPending>, where Output is your result type and WakerPending indicates the future isn't done yet.
Perfect, so I need to implement trait Future::poll for my executor struct. One last question: how do I handle multiple concurrent wakers if a single task can be notified by several different sources?

You store all active futures in the VecDeque and each has its own Arc<Weak> for independent notification. When any waker fires, it only signals that specific future; your executor then polls that one until completion before moving to others. You never need to track multiple sources per task unless you're implementing a custom scheduling logic.

Thanks! I think I have the core flow now: VecDeque of futures, Arc<Weak> for wakers, and a loop that polls one at a time. One more thing—I want to log which task completed when. How do I get access to the task's output from within the executor?

Each future in your VecDeque should implement trait Future::poll and return Ok(Output) where Output is whatever data you want to log. When poll returns Ok(output), that's when you can extract and log it before removing the task from the queue.

Hey, I've been staring at the exercises in this book about building a futures executor. I know what an executor is supposed to do conceptually, but I still don't fully understand how it actually works from scratch. It's giving me a headache because I feel like I'm missing something obvious.

Hey there! Welcome back to coding after five years. That feeling is totally normal when you're diving into async stuff again. The key here isn't just knowing the terms, but seeing how they fit together in a concrete system. Let's break down your specific exercise: building an executor that holds tasks in a VecDeque and uses Arc-based Wakers to poll them until completion.
Right, so the book says I need to store futures in a VecDeque. But why not just use channels or some other messaging pattern? And how exactly does polling work without blocking forever?

Great question on the channel part. Channels are great for communication between threads, but an executor is usually single-threaded and needs to manage state and wake-up logic internally. That's why we use a VecDeque of tasks; it lets us efficiently grab the next task without complex synchronization overhead compared to a full channel setup. As for polling, we don't sleep or block indefinitely. Instead, we maintain a Waker via Arc that gets notified whenever a future inside our deque is ready.
So the Waker via Arc is like a signal? But how do I actually implement that without just copying a generic pattern from somewhere else?

Exactly, it's a signal mechanism specific to your executor. You create an Arc<Weak<Mutex<...>>> so tasks can register their Waker by holding a Weak pointer, and the main loop holds the Strong reference. When a task completes, it calls the Wake method on that Arc, which triggers a wakeup of whoever is in the polling loop.
I see now, but I'm still stuck on how to actually poll until completion without infinite loops. The book mentions something about checking if all futures are done. How do I check that?

The polling logic is straightforward once you have the Waker setup. You loop while your VecDeque isn't empty, grab a task from the front, call its .poll method to try to advance it, and if that returns Ok(Ready), remove it and check again. If you get an error like Poll::Pending, you just re-poll later when the Waker fires.
Wait, so I'm calling .poll on each future individually? And what happens if a task fails? Does my executor just stop?

You are right about calling .poll on each future. If a task returns Err, that means it failed and your executor can either propagate the error or handle it gracefully depending on your design. Typically, you'd collect errors into an Option<Error> and stop polling once a failure occurs unless you want to retry specific tasks.
I'm starting to get it, but I'm still confused about how the Waker via Arc actually gets triggered during .poll. Is there some internal state change?

Yes, when a future's .poll returns Ok(Ready), that specific future has finished its work and is done being polled. The executor then checks if the task was ready to be woken up earlier via some internal state change or completion event. If so, it calls Waker::wake() on the Arc, which wakes up anyone holding a Weak pointer to the same Mutex.
So the executor is basically just a loop that polls tasks and triggers wakeups? That seems almost too simple compared to what I've seen in other frameworks.

That's exactly how it works at its core. Frameworks like tokio add abstractions on top of this, but your exercise is asking you to build the engine itself using a VecDeque and Waker via Arc. It's simpler than many frameworks because there's no scheduler or thread pool; just a single-threaded executor managing futures in a deque.
Okay, so I should start by setting up the VecDeque and then implement the Waker trait. But what type am I using for the shared state?

You're using an Arc<Weak<Mutex<VecDeque<F>>>> for the task storage so multiple tasks can safely register their Wakers without holding full ownership of the deque. The Mutex ensures thread safety if you ever expand this, but for your single-threaded version it's mostly about allowing weak references from futures.
I think I understand the structure now. One last thing though, how do I handle the case where a future never completes? Does my executor just hang forever?

If a future never returns Ok(Ready), your executor will keep polling it until some external event triggers a wakeup via Waker::wake. If you want to detect non-completion, you can track how many times .poll has returned Pending and set a timeout or maximum retry count before giving up.
Thanks! I think I have enough now to start coding. It's still tricky but the VecDeque + Waker approach makes sense once you see it in action.

You're very welcome! That's exactly how these things come together. Just keep testing small futures first and then scale up to more complex ones. The VecDeque + Waker via Arc pattern is the foundation you need here.
